sections.heading: Finding the Homes the Grid Forgot
sections.subtitle:
Using street view, aerial imagery and deep learning to identify Hard-to-Decarbonize houses
across Cambridge, UK.

sections.heading: Five Billion New City Dwellers
sections.body: By 2050, 80% of us will live in cities. Reaching net zero means decarbonizing the homes we already have — but the hardest-to-decarbonize quarter of them have been largely ignored.
sections.figure_alt: Random samples of street view and aerial view images from the Cambridge dataset

sections.heading: The Blind Spot in Net Zero
sections.body: “Hard-to-Decarbonize” (HtD) houses are estimated to be a quarter of all homes globally — older, narrow-cavity, off-gas, single-glazed. Policy exists; identification doesn't. Without knowing where these homes are, retrofit money goes everywhere except where it is needed most.
sections.figure_alt: Methodology overview: HtD identification, model training, model interpretation

sections.heading: Four Data Sources, One Verdict
sections.body: We fuse four publicly available data sources into a single deep learning model: street view images (what a passer-by sees), aerial view images (the roofs and footprints), land surface temperature (where heat leaks), and basic building stock attributes (age, area). Each branch contributes its own signal; the network learns how to combine them.
sections.figure_alt: Three-branch model architecture combining SVI, AVI and tabular features

sections.heading: How Each Data Source Performs
sections.body: Overall precision on 1,335 Cambridge houses — single-source ablation versus the full four-branch model.

sections.heading: What the Model Looks At
sections.body: Class Activation Maps reveal where the model focuses its attention. Across the dataset, it consistently picks out roofs, chimneys, windows and facade materials — the same architectural cues a building surveyor would use. It ignores the pavement, the cars and the trees.
sections.insight: Key Insight: The model isn't memorizing pixels — it learned to read buildings. That's why the same approach should travel beyond Cambridge.
sections.figure_alt: Class Activation Map overlays on street view images, red = high contribution

sections.heading: Two Lenses on the Same House
sections.body: Each data source picks up different architectural cues. The model's Class Activation Maps reveal what it actually looks at.

columns.heading: Street View
columns.body: Picks out windows, chimneys, facade material and ground-floor frontage — the same cues a surveyor would use during a doorstep visit.

columns.heading: Aerial View
columns.body: Reads roof geometry, footprint and surroundings. Less rich than street view, but covers most of the globe — a credible first map for data-poor regions.

sections.quote: With publicly available imagery and a building register, deep learning can map a city's hardest-to-decarbonize homes — at building-level resolution, without door-to-door surveys.
